title: Spaghetti alla Puttanesca
subtitle: Tomatoes, olives, capers, and anchovies — bold and fast
description: Puttanesca is a Neapolitan sauce built entirely from long-life storecupboard ingredients: tinned tomatoes, anchovies, black olives, capers, garlic, and chilli. The anchovies dissolve completely into the oil, leaving no fishiness — just depth and salt. The whole sauce cooks in the time it takes the pasta water to boil. It is one of the most practical pasta sauces in existence.
sourceNotes: First documented in Neapolitan cookery in the mid-twentieth century. The sauce is referenced by Marcella Hazan in Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking (1992) and has become a standard of Italian pasta repertoire.

recipeTools.notes: Wide enough to finish the spaghetti in and toss it.

glossaryTerms.definition: Salt-cured fish preserved in oil, sold in tins or jars. In cooked sauces they dissolve completely into the oil, contributing salt and umami without any overt fishy flavour.

steps: Bring a large pot of well-salted water to the boil. Add the spaghetti and cook until 1 minute short of the packet time.
While the pasta cooks, warm the olive oil in a large frying pan over a medium heat. Add the anchovy fillets and cook, stirring, for 1–2 minutes until they melt completely into the oil.
Add the sliced garlic and chilli flakes and cook for 1 minute until the garlic is just fragrant.
Add the crushed tomatoes, olives, and capers. Stir and cook for 10–12 minutes until the sauce has thickened. Taste — the anchovies and capers provide most of the salt, so you may need none.
Reserve a ladle of pasta water. Transfer the spaghetti to the sauce with tongs, add a splash of pasta water, and toss over a medium heat for 1 minute.
Remove from the heat, add the parsley, toss once more, and serve.

content.text: The salt in this dish comes from three sources — the anchovies, the capers, and the pasta water. Do not add extra salt to the sauce without tasting first, and under-salt the pasta water relative to usual. The sauce is self-seasoning.
Set the pasta water on to boil. Warm {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large frying pan over a medium heat. Add the chopped

and stir for 1–2 minutes — they will dissolve into the oil leaving nothing behind but flavour. Add the garlic and chilli flakes for 1 minute, then add the crushed tomatoes, chopped olives, and drained capers. Cook for 10–12 minutes until the sauce has thickened.

Cook {{spaghetti}} of spaghetti until 1 minute short of the packet time. Reserve a ladle of pasta water. Transfer the spaghetti to the sauce with tongs, add a splash of water, and toss over a medium heat for 1 minute. Taste for seasoning — you will likely need no extra salt. Add the parsley, toss, and serve.

The anchovies vanish completely during cooking. The result does not taste of fish — it tastes savoury, deep, and salty in a way that is difficult to pinpoint. If serving to guests who usually avoid anchovies, you do not need to mention them; most people cannot detect them once cooked.
